source_paper_title: Specific Wasserstein Divergence Between Continuous Martingales

context:
Page 7, Section 2.1, Remark 2.4.

We consider the scaling limit of (2.1) for our choice \(\alpha_1(\cdot)=\pm W_1(\cdot,P)^p\) as an interesting open question which we do not tackle here.
title: Scaling limit of dynamic risk measures with Wasserstein-type one-step penalties
background_latex:
Let \(\Omega:=C([0,1];\mathbb{R})\) be the canonical continuous-path space with canonical process \(X_t(\omega)=\omega_t\). Fix \(p>0\) and a reference law \(P\in\mathcal{P}(\Omega)\) (typically a martingale law).

For each \(N\in\mathbb{N}\), let \((Q)^N\in\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R}^N)\) denote the law of \((X_{1/N},\dots,X_{N/N})\) under \(Q\in\mathcal{P}(\Omega)\), and similarly for \((P)^N\).

For probability measures \(Q,P\in\mathcal{P}_1(\mathbb{R}^N)\) with suitable regular conditional distributions, define the time-consistent multistep discrepancy (additive chain construction)
\[
D^{W}_{1,p}(Q\|\|P):=W_1(Q,P)^p,
\]
\[
D^{W}_{N,p}(Q\|\|P):=D^{W}_{N-1,p}(Q_{1:N-1}\|\|P_{1:N-1})+\int W_1\bigl(Q^{x_{1:N-1}}_N,\,P^{x_{1:N-1}}_N\bigr)^p\,dQ_{1:N-1}(x_{1:N-1}),
\]
where \(W_1\) is the 1-Wasserstein distance on \(\mathbb{R}\), \(Q_{1:N-1}\) is the projection onto the first \(N-1\) coordinates, and \(Q^{x_{1:N-1}}_N\) denotes the conditional law of the \(N\)th coordinate given \(x_{1:N-1}\) (and similarly for \(P\)).

Let \(\rho_N\) be the dynamic (multi-period) risk measure with penalty function built from a one-step penalty \(\alpha_1\) via additive decomposition: \(\alpha_N(Q)=\int \sum_{i=1}^N \alpha_1(Q_i^{x_{1:i-1}})\,dQ\). The paper considers in particular the one-step penalty \(\alpha_1(\cdot)=\pm W_1(\cdot,P_1)^{p}\), yielding an objective of the general form
\[
\inf_{H\ \text{predictable}} \rho_N\bigl(F_N(X)-(H\cdot X)_N\bigr)
\]
whose formal minimax dual involves \(D^{W}_{N,p}((Q)^N\|\|(P)^N)\) (up to sign/convention).

The paper identifies the scaling \(N^{p/2-1}D^{W}_{N,p}((Q)^N\|\|(P)^N)\) as the correct continuous-time order for martingale laws, and proves existence/identification of the corresponding limit for a specific divergence functional, but does not analyze the scaling limit of the associated dynamic risk measure/pricing functional \(\inf_H\rho_N(\cdot)\) itself when \(\alpha_1(\cdot)=\pm W_1(\cdot,P)^{p}\).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine the scaling limit, as \(N\to\infty\), of the multi-period optimization problem
\[
\inf_{H\ \mathrm{predictable}}\ \rho_N\bigl(F_N(X)-(H\cdot X)_N\bigr)
\]
when the one-step penalty of \(\rho_N\) is given by \(\alpha_1(q)=\pm W_1(q, P_1)^p\), i.e., when the induced \(N\)-step penalty is the additive functional corresponding to \(D^{W}_{N,p}\), and identify the correct normalization in \(N\) (if any) and the resulting continuous-time limit functional on laws \(Q\in\mathcal{P}(\Omega)\).
latex:

open_problem_quote: We consider the scaling limit of (2.1) for our choice \(\alpha_1(\cdot)=\pm W_1(\cdot,P)^p\) as an interesting open question which we do not tackle here.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement asks for an identification of an asymptotic limit functional, not a yes/no property that can be refuted by a finite instance. While one can numerically explore candidate scalings for specific choices of \(F_N\) and \(P\), such experiments would not constitute a decisive counterexample in generality.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: For fixed families of payoffs \(F_N\), reference models \(P\), and discretizations, an evolutionary code loop could search for the correct normalization and conjectured limit form by fitting asymptotics and testing on many simulated martingale models. However, turning this into a general mathematical characterization likely requires theory beyond computable fitness feedback.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper introduces the chain-constructed multistep discrepancy \(D^{W}_{N,p}\) built from one-step \(W_1^p\) costs and identifies the correct normalization \(N^{p/2-1}\) for continuous-time limits under martingale laws. It proves that for continuous martingales with absolutely continuous quadratic variation, \(N^{p/2-1}D^{W}_{N,p}((Q)^N\|\|(P)^N)\) converges to an explicit integral functional of the volatility mismatch, e.g. \(\mathrm{SW}^p(Q\|P)=(2/\pi)^{p/2}\,\mathbb E^Q[\int_0^1||\sigma_t|-|\eta_t||^p\,dt]\) for diffusion-martingale reference \(P\).

However, the open problem concerns the scaling limit of the associated time-consistent dynamic risk measure / robust hedging functional \(\inf_H\rho_N(F_N-(H\cdot X)_N)\) when the one-step penalty is \(\alpha_1(q)=\pm W_1(q,P_1)^p\). The forward citations provided do not resolve this: the reciprocal specific relative entropy paper develops a related continuous-time control problem and links entropy to the \(p\downarrow2\) derivative of the specific \(p\)-Wasserstein divergence, while the multidimensional specific relative entropy paper provides an entropy analogue of the scaling-limit-to-quadratic-variation paradigm. Thus, based on the available forward-citation evidence, the discrete-to-continuous scaling limit for the full dynamic risk measure/pricing functional remains open.

sources.title: Reciprocal Specific Relative Entropy between Continuous Martingales
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper introduces the reciprocal specific relative entropy between a continuous martingale law \(Q\) and Wiener measure \(W\),
\[h(Q\|W)=\tfrac12\,\mathbb E^Q\Big[\int_0^1 \big(\Sigma_t\log\Sigma_t+1-\Sigma_t\big)\,dt\Big],\]
where \(\Sigma_t=d\langle X\rangle_t/dt\). It proves (Theorem 3.1) that, over the class \(\mathcal M^{\mathrm{win}}_{t,x}\) of win-martingales on \([t,1]\) starting at \(x\in(0,1)\) and ending in \(\{0,1\}\) at time 1, the unique minimizer of \(\inf_{Q\in\mathcal M^{\mathrm{win}}_{t,x}} \tfrac12\mathbb E^Q[\int_t^1 \Sigma_u\log\Sigma_u\,du]\) is the scaled neutral Wright–Fisher diffusion \(dM_s=\sqrt{\frac{M_s(1-M_s)}{1-s}}\,dB_s\), and the value function is explicit. It also shows this objective equals the derivative at \(p=2\) of the specific \(p\)-Wasserstein divergence objective \(\inf_{Q\in\mathcal M^{\mathrm{win}}_{0,x}}\mathbb E^Q[\int_0^1 \Sigma_t^{p/2}\,dt]\) under an integrability condition.
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for the scaling limit of a discrete-time dynamic risk measure/hedging functional whose penalty is built from one-step \(\pm W_1(\cdot,P_1)^p\), i.e. the additive multistep Wasserstein-type discrepancy \(D^W_{N,p}\), including the correct normalization \(N^{p/2-1}\), and identification of the limiting continuous-time functional on path laws \(Q\in\mathcal P(\Omega)\).

This citing paper does not analyze the discrete-time dynamic risk measure \(\inf_H\rho_N(F_N-(H\cdot X)_N)\) nor the chain-constructed \(D^W_{N,p}((Q)^N\|\|(P)^N)\) scaling limit. Instead it studies a different continuous-time divergence functional \(h(Q\|W)\) (an entropy in quadratic variation) and shows it arises as the \(p\downarrow2\) derivative of the *specific* \(p\)-Wasserstein divergence from [10], which itself is a continuous-time scaling limit object. Thus the paper is relevant mainly as a related tool/insight: it identifies a canonical second-order (critical \(p=2\)) refinement/selection criterion for the specific Wasserstein divergence, and provides PDE/control methods and explicit optimizers in a calibration problem.

So it does not solve the stated scaling-limit problem for the dynamic risk measure with one-step Wasserstein penalties, but it may inform what the limiting penalty could look like near \(p=2\) (entropy-type correction) and suggests techniques (HJB/viscosity) for continuous-time limits of discrete-time robust pricing problems.

sources.title: Multidimensional specific relative entropy between continuous martingales
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper defines the multidimensional specific relative entropy between continuous martingale laws by the scaling limit 
\[h_l(Q\mid P):=\liminf_{n\to\infty}2^{-n}H(Q\mid P)\big|_{\mathcal F_{2^n}},\]
and proves (multidimensional Gantert inequality) that for a square-integrable \(\mathbb R^l\)-valued \(Q\)-martingale with absolutely continuous quadratic covariation \(d\langle X\rangle_t/dt=\Sigma_t^Q\),
\[h_l(Q\mid B^l)\ge \mathbb E^Q\Big[\int_0^1 F_l(\Sigma_t^Q)\,dt\Big],\qquad F_l(\Sigma)=\tfrac12(\mathrm{tr}(\Sigma)-l-\log\det\Sigma).\]
It further shows this lower bound is tight in the convex-analytic sense: the functional \(Q\mapsto \mathbb E^Q[\int_0^1 F_l(\Sigma_t^Q)dt]\) is the convex lower semicontinuous envelope of \(Q\mapsto h_l(Q\mid B^l)\).
sources.connection: The open problem concerns scaling limits of dynamic risk measures whose discrete-time penalty is an additive chain of one-step \(W_1^p\) costs, i.e. a specific Wasserstein-type divergence \(D^W_{N,p}\) with normalization \(N^{p/2-1}\), and asks for the limiting continuous-time risk/pricing functional \(\inf_H\rho_N(\cdot)\). This citing paper instead studies a different divergence (relative entropy) and its scaling limit \(2^{-n}H(\cdot\mid\cdot)\) for continuous martingales, proving inequalities and convex envelopes in terms of quadratic variation. It does not analyze Wasserstein penalties, the chain-constructed \(D^W_{N,p}\), nor any dynamic risk measure optimization \(\inf_H\rho_N\). Thus it does not solve the stated open problem, but provides background/analogous results for another divergence where the scaling limit yields an integral functional of quadratic variation.
